segments.title: Writing treaty rules without inviting evasions
segments.summary: A question about creative accounting leads to the problem of making naval treaty rules enforceable.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that states will look for ways around restrictions, so excessive specificity can create fresh loopholes. His equitable-treaty proposal therefore favours broader limits over intricate rules that practically require signatories to evade them.
segments.question: How could an equitable treaty work if participants used creative accounting?
segments.answerShort: No rule set eliminates evasion, but broad limits are less likely to manufacture loopholes than highly detailed restrictions.

evidence.note: He contrasts broad treaty rules with detailed provisions that invite creative accounting.

segments.title: Replacement cycles instead of preserving Hood
segments.summary: The proposed treaty would encourage iterative replacement designs rather than keeping Hood and Repulse indefinitely.
segments.body: The answer treats Hood's original structure as a hard limit on modernization. With a continuing construction cycle, Britain could replace aging ships progressively and learn from successive treaty battleship designs instead of waiting for one late generation.
segments.question: How would Britain make Hood, Repulse, and Prince of Wales as capable as possible under this treaty?
segments.answerShort: Hood would ultimately be replaced, while recurring construction would produce successive improved designs rather than one final treaty generation.

evidence.note: He explains Hood's design limits and an iterative replacement process.

segments.title: Why battlecruisers do not make battleships obsolete
segments.summary: Speed lets a battlecruiser avoid an unfavourable fight, but it cannot replace a battleship's protection.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes choosing the engagement from surviving in the battle line. A battlecruiser can use speed and manoeuvre to avoid a battleship, but a hit is more damaging because it lacks equivalent armour; it needs repeated good fortune to win.
segments.question: Would Hood make older American battleships obsolete?
segments.answerShort: No. A battlecruiser can avoid a battleship, but it does not have the protection to make battleships obsolete in a direct fight.

evidence.note: He explains the battlecruiser's speed advantage and vulnerability to hits.

segments.title: Security, not equal weakness, as treaty stability
segments.summary: A treaty lasts only if its signatories feel able to defend themselves under it.
segments.body: The discussion rejects the idea that simply making fleets weaker guarantees peace. Dr. Clarke argues that disarmament must be universal or every state needs enough strength to feel secure, with force levels shaped by what each state considers defensible.
segments.question: Could an equitable naval treaty have guaranteed peace?
segments.answerShort: No treaty guarantees peace, but one is more durable when participants feel secure rather than permanently weakened.

evidence.note: He contrasts equal weakness with security sufficient for self-defence.

segments.title: The limits of rebuilding old capital ships
segments.summary: Older hulls can be improved, but their internal arrangements limit how far modernization can keep pace.
segments.body: Answering a proposal to retain and convert older battlecruisers, Dr. Clarke stresses that new machinery and equipment cannot wholly overcome an older ship's internal design. Modernization remains useful for specialised roles, but it cannot make every old capital ship a peer of newly designed vessels.
segments.question: Would retaining and oil-converting older battlecruisers be better than keeping older battleships?
segments.answerShort: It could be interesting, but modernization cannot fully overcome an old hull's original internal and technological limits.

evidence.note: He explains why internal design constrains reconstruction.

segments.title: A 10:7 ratio and Japanese security
segments.summary: The proposed 10:7 ratio aims to bind larger fleets while avoiding Japan's sense of permanent inferiority.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a 10:7 ratio would still acknowledge Japan's industrial disadvantage in a prolonged arms race, but would be less humiliating than 5:3. The intended effect is deterrence through sufficient security, not an illusion of equal war-winning power.
segments.question: Why would the equitable treaty be less likely to collapse in the 1930s?
segments.answerShort: A 10:7 ratio could make Japan feel secure while still constraining the United States and Britain, unlike a ratio that made Japan feel permanently weak.

evidence.note: He compares the security effects of 10:7 and 5:3 ratios.

segments.title: How national priorities shape treaty battleships
segments.summary: Britain, the United States, and Japan would make different speed, protection, and construction choices under the same limits.
segments.body: The answer explores a British programme of fast battleships that keeps shipyards active, a slower and cheaper American preference constrained by Congress, and a Japanese desire to wait and tailor a clean-sheet response. It shows how the guns-armour-speed trade-off depends on strategic and political context rather than tonnage alone.
segments.question: What capital-ship programmes would Britain, the United States, and Japan pursue under the equitable treaty?
segments.answerShort: Britain would likely favour fast ships and steady construction, the United States might trade speed for cost and protection, and Japan would wait to tailor its response.

evidence.note: He compares British, American, and Japanese design and procurement incentives.

segments.title: Tiger as a fast specialist rather than a battle-line ship
segments.summary: A rebuilt Tiger could retain value as a fast cruiser-hunter even while older battleships become liabilities.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees a highly modernized Tiger as more useful than retaining older Queen Elizabeth-class ships in the battle line. Speed gives a rebuilt battlecruiser a distinct scouting, cruiser-protection, and deterrent role, whereas slow non-treaty capital ships would be outclassed outside the Atlantic.
segments.question: Would rebuilding Tiger be a better use of resources than retaining older British battleships?
segments.answerShort: Potentially. A very fast Tiger would have specialised utility, while old slow battleships would be dangerous to retain in a modern battle line.

evidence.note: He contrasts a modernized Tiger with older battle-line capital ships.

segments.title: Balancing cruiser limits and unfinished battleships
segments.summary: Lower cruiser limits might appease peace factions while still leaving room to complete some unfinished American capital ships.
segments.body: This exchange weighs a roughly 40,000-ton battleship ceiling against lower heavy-cruiser limits. Dr. Clarke notes that the political appeal of saving sunk costs could make completion of some South Dakota hulls plausible, depending on the permitted construction schedule.
segments.question: Could lower heavy-cruiser limits coexist with completing the American South Dakotas?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, if the construction timetable allowed it and Congress preferred finishing partly built ships to starting anew.

evidence.note: He discusses cruiser limits and completing already-started South Dakota ships.

segments.title: Why automatic escalation has a practical ceiling
segments.summary: A treaty that automatically increases tonnage and gun calibre would soon reach the practical limit of capital-ship weapons.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke considers a proposal for annual increases in displacement and gun calibre. He argues that it would quickly converge on 18-inch guns and the tonnages required to carry them, after which further escalation offers little practical value.
segments.question: Would a treaty with scheduled annual increases in ship size and gun calibre be more realistic?
segments.answerShort: It is an interesting idea, but it rapidly reaches the practical ceiling around 18-inch guns and very large displacements.

evidence.note: He explains how scheduled escalation quickly reaches 18-inch guns.

segments.title: Battlecruisers for trade protection and cruiser hunting
segments.summary: Fast capital ships retain a role against commerce raiders and cruisers, but cannot substitute for the whole battle fleet.
segments.body: The discussion identifies trade protection, interdiction, and cruiser hunting as reasons for Britain or Japan to retain battlecruisers. It also argues that a new construction cycle would make most pre-treaty battleships obsolete by the Second World War, with selective battlecruiser modernization more plausible.
segments.question: Would battlecruisers still have a role in a treaty system built around new capital ships?
segments.answerShort: Yes, especially for trade protection and cruiser hunting, but most old battle-line ships would soon be outclassed.

evidence.note: He discusses trade roles and the likely obsolescence of older capital ships.

segments.title: Trading speed and protection for a 16-inch battery
segments.summary: A 16-inch treaty standard creates a choice between a conventional nine-gun ship and a larger twelve-gun design with compromises.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the familiar guns, armour, and speed relationship as unavoidable. Nine 16-inch guns in triple turrets would be the practical baseline, while twelve guns or a 35-knot rebuilt battlecruiser demand substantial trade-offs in protection, machinery, or hull form.
segments.question: Could a 16-inch standard support twelve-gun ships or 35-knot rebuilt battlecruisers?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, but both demand difficult compromises; a nine-gun triple-turret arrangement is the more natural baseline.

evidence.note: He weighs nine versus twelve 16-inch guns and the difficulty of reaching 35 knots.
